title: Weather-driven congestion event — storm of 16–17 April 2024 (Al Maktoum Bridge)

map._comment: Featured corridor MAK_N1 (Al Maktoum Bridge, D75, Bur Dubai) selected as the most storm-impacted of the task candidate set {SZR_N1, GAR_N1, MAK_N1, ITT_W1} — largest mean-speed collapse on 16 Apr 2024. A flood-prone bridge whose low storm-day vc but severe slowdown is a clean weather-incident signature; also diverse from the Case 1 flagship.

triage.narrative: On 16 April 2024, Al Maktoum Bridge (MAK_N1) experienced severe congestion with 4.17% of hours at gridlock (LOS F), despite a low mean volume-to-capacity ratio of 0.379. The corridor saw an average unmet demand of 357 vehicles per hour, but the 49.7% drop in mean speed (from 55.3 to 27.8 kph) at such low demand confirms this was a weather-driven incident, not a capacity issue.
triage._comment: STORM-DAY values, 16 Apr 2024 only (24 hrs) — not 3-year means. rank = 4 of 18 by mean-speed collapse on the storm day (MAK_N1 −49.7%); it leads the task candidate set {SZR_N1, GAR_N1, MAK_N1, ITT_W1}, while three non-candidate leisure/outlier corridors (JBR_X1, DWC_X1, BBC_S1, all off a low demand base) dropped more.

diagnosis.summary: This weather incident on Al Maktoum Bridge (MAK_N1) on 16–17 April 2024 was caused by heavy precipitation (200 mm total, peak 28.5 mm/h), reduced visibility, and a surge in incidents. The corridor’s mean speed collapsed by 49.7% while the volume-to-capacity ratio remained low (0.379), indicating congestion was due to weather, not demand. Incident response times doubled to 24.6 minutes during the storm window.
evidence: MAK_N1 storm-day (16 Apr 2024) mean vc_ratio 0.379 while mean speed fell 49.7% (55.3→27.8 kph, min 8.1 kph) — severe slowdown at low v/c confirms congestion is weather- not demand-driven
Heavy precipitation 16–17 April 2024: 200 mm total (peak 28.5 mm/h), thunderstorm, visibility down to 1 km
Incident cluster during the storm window
Storm-window response time 24.6 min vs 9.7 min 3-year baseline (2.5x); 61 of 96 incidents on the first day

simulate._comment: No fix candidates for a transient weather event — the response is operational (incident clearance, diversion, hard-shoulder running), not a capacity/signal intervention. candidates intentionally empty.

rank.recommendation: No infrastructure or signal fixes are applicable for this transient weather event. Focus on operational responses: prioritize incident clearance, implement diversion plans, and consider hard-shoulder running during storms. Human review is required to validate these measures, as the diagnosis confidence is 55% and no estimable candidates exist for this case.

flow_animation._comment: Storm-day vs normal observed mean speeds for MAK_N1 (ff=60 kph): before = 16 Apr 2024 storm day (mean 27.8 kph), after = normal non-storm 2024 baseline (mean 55.3 kph). speed_factor = mean speed / ff. Density reflects observed slow/jammed movement; note vc stayed LOW (0.379) — the weather signature is severe slowdown at low volume.

beats: Rainfall peaked at 28.5 mm per hour.
annotations.after: Pre-stage crews: cut storm response below 24.6 minutes

plain.headline: Storm on 16–17 April 2024 crippled Al Maktoum Bridge despite low traffic volumes.
plain.los_f: At gridlock (LOS F) roughly 1 hour in every 24.
plain.gap: About 357 drivers every hour want this road but cannot fit.
plain.vc: Averages about 38% of its capacity, far higher at peaks.
